Alright, you're back. Let's get rolling. So the best way to teach you this is to actually do a little bit of role-playing with myself. I'm going to pretend I'm actually pitching you. So let me borrow a language pattern that I typically use to close people. So I would say something along the lines of, you know, John, you give me one shot and believe me the only problem you'll have is that you didn't buy more. Sound fair enough? That's a very basic language pattern. It could also be, John, you give me one shot and believe me, you're going to be very, very impressed. Sound fair enough or sound good or John, you give me one shot and believe me, the only problem you'll have is it didn't call you six months ago and get you started then. Sound fair enough? You get the words, all the words, right? But what I want you're now keen on here is the tonalities I use to close. Now let's go back to the beginning here to, you know, John, you give me one shot and believe me. So that first chunk, John, you give me one shot. That's the tonality of absolute certainty. Notice how my body language is tense. I'm like, John, you give me one shot. I'm not like, John, give me one shot. I'm like, John, you give me one shot. The tonality of certainty is not necessarily a yelling loud tonality. It could be even a very soft, a whisper. It's oomph behind it. John, you give me one shot. It's almost like you're receiving volcano. There's a power inside you. Comes from your solar plexus that when you just hear these words, it just, wow, like, he must really mean that. He believes it. So it's like, you know, John, you give me one shot. Not John, you give me one shot. That's not certainty. John, you give me one shot. John, name of the company. See, it applies many places throughout the presentation. But for right now, when I close, I always do it by, John, you give me one shot. And my transition now is, and believe me. And I collapse to this second tonality, what's called utter sincerity. That's your second tonality, utter sincerity. So John, you give me one shot and believe me. The only problem you'll have is that you didn't buy more. Now notice how I'm now calm, silky smooth, no pressure whatsoever. The last thing you want to do when you close, say, John, you give me one shot and believe me, you'll be very, very impressed. Sound fair enough to be like, no, that doesn't sound too fair to me. It sounds like you're out of your freaking mind, right? So you never would close like that. So you want to have this, you know, this certainty built up, right? So you'll, John, you give me one shot and believe me. The only problem you'll have is you didn't buy more. Utterson, the only problem you'll have, utter sincerity, friend to friend, no pressures. The only problem you have is you didn't buy more. That's the silky smooth tonality of utter sincerity. So John, you give me one shot, absolute certainty and believe me. The only problem you have is you didn't buy more. Sound fair enough. And those last three words are called the reasonable man tone. In other words, sound fair enough, I'm reasonable. You're reasonable. It's like you're almost saying it in a way like, you know, it's such a reasonable pressure. I mean, I'm not saying sound fair enough. Like, you know, sound fair enough. Come on, John. It's like the old golden rule, you know, if someone's reasonable to you, you're reasonable to them back. Well, I'm like, come on, John. It's really a reasonable thing to do here. So that tonality really implies that I'm really making a pretty reasonable request between two people who trust each other. So you know, John, you give me one shot and believe me, you'll be impressed. Sound fair. You're like, no, don't run. You run for the door. Right? But watch, John, you give me one shot so I, you know, I'm certain. Like I'm really excited. Believe me. The only problem you'll have is you didn't buy more. Sound fair enough. It's like up to sound fair enough. I'm reasonable. You're reasonable. And those are those extra words that your prospect hears. One more time. Right? So you have three tonalities stacked together. I say, John, you give me one shot. And when you're saying that, what the prospect is, yeah, this guy really means it. He's pretty serious about this. You give me one shot and then you say, and believe me. Now any piece, I can expect the pressure and you diffuse the pressure and believe me, you'll be very, very impressed. And believe me, you might be kissing you when she walks through the door or she'll be kissing you when you walk through the door. Sound fair enough. Up down. Sound fair enough. Reasonable man. When you stack those three together and then close and you have someone at a higher level sort of do, wow, just watch what happens. You get people who are on the fence. They used to call it fairy dust back in the day. So I'd be in the bottom of Stratton and you'd have these guys that were close to close. They couldn't quite get the prospect to say yes, they were very close. And they'd say, come sprinkle fairy dust on me, seriously. And I'd walk over and I'd actually feed them lines. And mostly it was about getting into the spot and saying, believe me, you'll be glad you did it. Sound good. And shut up. All right. So those are three tonalities. Let's take a short quiz here. Make sure you got those locked in. Do your exercises in the resource center and we'll rock on. I'll see you in the next lesson.
